company_profile.name: Youxin Technology Ltd
company_profile.type_rationale: Standard company — balanced weighting across all methods
company_profile.description: Youxin Technology Ltd operates as a cloud-based software as a service (SaaS) and platform as a service (PaaS) provider in Mainland China. Its cloud-based SaaS product and PaaS platform help retail enterprises digitally transform their businesses to develop, use, and control business applications. The company's products optimizes the overall supply chain by streamlining the decision-making process, efficiency, and profitability. It sells and markets its products directly to customers and third-party agents. The company was founded in 2018 and is headquartered in Guangzhou, the People's Republic of China.
company_profile.description_summary: This company provides cloud-based SaaS and PaaS solutions enabling retail enterprises in Mainland China to digitally transform their operations. Its platform helps businesses develop and manage applications while optimizing supply chain efficiency, decision-making, and profitability through direct sales and third-party agents.

_notes.fcf_vs_debt: Free cash flow is the cash left over after the company has paid for everything it needs to run and grow the business. This chart compares that cash to the company's total debt. A company with rising free cash flow and falling debt is in a strong financial position. If debt is rising faster than cash, the company may be taking on more risk than it can comfortably manage.
_notes.shares_vs_buybacks: When a company buys back its own shares, it reduces the total number of shares in circulation — which means each remaining share represents a larger slice of the business. This chart shows how the share count has changed over time. Consistent buybacks are generally a sign that management believes the stock is undervalued and wants to return cash to shareholders.
_notes.returns_trajectory: Return on Equity (ROE) measures how much profit a company generates for every dollar of shareholder money invested. Return on Invested Capital (ROIC) is similar but also includes debt. Both are expressed as a percentage — higher is better. A company consistently earning above 15% ROIC is generally considered to have a genuine competitive advantage over its peers.
_notes.revenue_vs_ar: Accounts receivable is money the company is owed by customers who have not yet paid their bills. This chart compares revenue growth to accounts receivable growth each year. If accounts receivable is growing much faster than revenue, it may mean the company is struggling to collect payments — sometimes a sign of aggressive or fictitious revenue reporting. A healthy company should see both lines move at a similar pace.

company_profile.name: Yunhong Green CTI Ltd.
company_profile.type_rationale: Standard company — balanced weighting across all methods
company_profile.description: Yunhong Green CTI Ltd. develops, produces, distributes, and sells consumer products in the United States and internationally. The company offers novelty products, including foil and latex balloons, and related gift items; and inflatable latex items, which include punch balls, water bombs, and balloons twisted into shapes, and other specialty designs. It also provides flexible films for food, and other commercial and packaging applications, as well as assembles and sells Candy Blossom product line. The company serves various retail outlets, including general merchandise stores, discount and drugstore chains, grocery chains, card and gift shops, party goods stores, and florists and balloon decorators. It sells its products directly, as well as through a network of distributors and wholesalers, retail chains, and independent sales representatives. The company was formerly known as Yunhong CTI Ltd. and changed its name to Yunhong Green CTI Ltd. in August 2023. Yunhong Green CTI Ltd. was incorporated in 1983 and is headquartered in Lake Barrington, Illinois.
company_profile.description_summary:
The company develops and manufactures consumer products including novelty items like foil and latex balloons, inflatable toys, and flexible packaging films for food and commercial applications. It distributes these products globally through retail chains, specialty stores, distributors, and sales representatives.

The company offers a diverse product portfolio spanning party goods, gift items, and the Candy Blossom product line, serving multiple retail channels from discount stores to specialty florists and balloon decorators.

company_profile.name: 111, Inc.
company_profile.type_rationale: Standard company — balanced weighting across all methods
company_profile.description: 111, Inc., together with its subsidiaries, operates an integrated online and offline platform in the healthcare market in the People's Republic of China. It operates through two segments, B2C and B2B. The company offers supply chain integration services that help pharmaceutical companies manage products through online and offline channels; product promotion, customer analytics, patient education, and brand awareness services; and marketing and channel data services. It also provides 1 Pharmacy online wholesale pharmacy that offers pharmaceutical and other health and wellness products; and 1 Medicine Marketplace online retail pharmacy that provides drugs, nutritional supplements, medical supplies and devices, personal care, and baby products. In addition, the company offers online loan application services; cloud-based inventory management services; smart procurement services, which collect pharmacies' historical purchase orders and inventory data; and Hawkeye automated salesforce tool, as well as online consultation, e-prescription, and data services. Further, its pharmacies provide drugs, including prescription and over-the-counter drugs comprising Western and Chinese medicines; nutritional supplements, such as vitamins and dietary products; contact lenses; medical supplies and devices that consists of bandages and thermometers; and personal care products that include skin care, birth control, sexual wellness, and baby products. Additionally, the company offers research and development, and consulting services; warehousing, procurement, and logistics services; and software development and information technology support services. It serves pharmacies, pharmaceutical companies, medical professionals, and marketplace sellers. The company was formerly known as New Peak Group and changed its name to 111, Inc. in April 2018. 111, Inc. was founded in 2010 and is headquartered in Shanghai, the People's Republic of China.
company_profile.description_summary:
Operates an integrated online and offline healthcare platform in China through B2C and B2B segments. Revenue derives from digital pharmacy retail (1 Pharmacy wholesale, 1 Medicine Marketplace), supply chain services for pharmaceutical companies, and B2B solutions including inventory management and data analytics for pharmacies and drugmakers.

Distinctive offerings include loan application services, automated salesforce tools, e-prescription capabilities, and comprehensive physical pharmacy operations providing prescription drugs, supplements, and health products alongside digital services.

company_profile.name: The York Water Company
company_profile.type_rationale: Regulated utility — P/E and EV/EBITDA prioritised; high debt is structural
company_profile.description: The York Water Company impounds, purifies, and distributes drinking water. It owns and operates three wastewater collection systems; twelve wastewater collection and treatment systems; and two reservoirs, including Lake Williams and Lake Redman, which hold approximately 2.5 billion gallons of water. The company operates a 15-mile pipeline from the Susquehanna River to Lake Redman; and owns satellite groundwater systems in York, Adams, and Lancaster Counties, as well as two impounding dams on primary system located in York and Springfield Townships. It serves customers in the fixtures and furniture, electrical machinery, food products, paper, ordnance units, textile products, air conditioning systems, laundry detergents, barbells, and motorcycle industries in 58 municipalities within four counties in south-central Pennsylvania. The York Water Company was incorporated in 1816 and is based in York, Pennsylvania.
company_profile.description_summary:
This utility company impounds, purifies, and distributes drinking water across south-central Pennsylvania, serving 58 municipalities in four counties. It operates two reservoirs, three wastewater collection systems, twelve combined collection and treatment systems, and satellite groundwater operations, generating revenue primarily through water distribution and wastewater services.

The company maintains significant infrastructure including two impounding dams and a 15-mile pipeline from the Susquehanna River, positioning it as a comprehensive water management provider for diverse industrial and municipal customers.

_meta.type_rationale: Regulated utility — P/E and EV/EBITDA prioritised; high debt is structural
_meta.method_explanation: Utilities like electricity and water companies operate under government regulation, which means their prices and profits are capped but also very stable. They tend to carry a lot of debt because building infrastructure is expensive — but this is normal and expected for the industry. Because earnings are so predictable, Price-to-Earnings (P/E) and EV/EBITDA work well. DCF is less useful here because regulated utilities have limited ability to grow beyond what regulators allow.

_meta.type_rationale: FCF compounder — FCF margin 35.9%; DCF methods prioritised
_meta.method_explanation: Free cash flow compounders are businesses that consistently generate more cash than they need to run the business — think of companies like consumer goods brands or software firms with subscription revenue. Because their cash flows are reliable year after year, a Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model works very well. DCF asks: if I add up all the future cash flows this company will generate and adjust for the time value of money, what is it worth today? For these companies, that calculation gives a meaningful and trustworthy answer.

company_profile.type_rationale: Cyclical business (energy) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
company_profile.description: YPF Sociedad Anónima, an energy company, engages in the oil and gas upstream and downstream activities in South America and Argentina. The company operates through the Upstream, Midstream and Downstream, LNG and Integrated Gas, and New Energies segments. It is involved in the exploration and exploitation of hydrocarbon fields and production of crude oil and natural gas; the refining, transportation and commercialization of refined products; the production, transportation, and commercialization of petrochemical products; the transportation and commercialization of crude oil; and the commercialization of specialties for the agribusiness industry, and of grains and their by-products. The company was incorporated in 1977 and is based in Buenos Aires, Argentina.
company_profile.description_summary:
An energy company operating across South America and Argentina, YPF engages in oil and gas exploration, production, refining, and transportation. It generates revenue from crude oil, natural gas, refined products, petrochemicals, and specialty agribusiness products including fertilizers, grains, and crop protection solutions.

The company distinctively integrates upstream hydrocarbon extraction with downstream refining and commercialization, while also developing liquefied natural gas capacity and power generation activities.

_meta.type_rationale: Cyclical business (energy) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
_meta.method_explanation: Cyclical companies — such as semiconductors, mining, or manufacturing — have profits that rise sharply in good times and fall sharply in downturns. This makes earnings-based methods like P/E unreliable, because the earnings figure at any single point in time can be unusually high or low. EV/EBITDA smooths out some of this distortion by looking at earnings before interest and tax, making it a fairer comparison across the economic cycle. Price-to-Sales is also useful as revenue tends to be more stable than profits for cyclical businesses.

Revenue primarily comes from franchising fees and restaurant operations spanning chicken, Mexican-style food, pizza, and chargrilled burgers.

The company's distinctive strength lies in its multi-brand portfolio approach, allowing it to serve diverse customer preferences and geographic markets through specialized concepts rather than relying on a single brand.

quality_narrative.moat_rationale: Yum! Brands commands a strong competitive moat built on iconic franchise brands, exceptional capital efficiency (31% ROIC), and sticky franchise agreements, though modest predictability and valuation concerns prevent it from reaching elite status.

metric_assessments.profitability: A 20.5% profit margin places Yum! well above the 10–15% range typical of consumer businesses, reflecting the high-margin nature of its franchise model where franchisees bear most operating costs. The company extracts substantial earnings from brand royalties and fees without heavy direct expenses, which is the core strength of this model. However, the 7/10 score reflects that margins, while strong, aren't at the exceptional 25%+ levels seen in companies with pricing power over decades—Yum! must still compete on value and menu innovation.
metric_assessments.financial_strength: With a current ratio of 1.35 and debt-to-EBITDA of 4.39, Yum! carries moderate leverage that is manageable but not conservative—most stable blue-chip companies stay below 3.5x. The franchise model generates reliable cash to service debt, which explains why this scores as 'Good' rather than concerning, yet the leverage load exceeds what you'd see in a fortress balance sheet. This level leaves less room for unexpected downturns before financial stress becomes a real issue.
metric_assessments.growth: A 15.2% revenue growth rate is solid and above the 5–10% long-term average for mature food franchises, while the 11.4% five-year EPS growth forecast is respectable for a company already operating thousands of restaurants globally. What holds this back from higher marks is the notation that growth is constrained—Yum! is bumping against the limits of how fast it can expand its restaurant footprint and same-store sales without market saturation, meaning explosive acceleration is unlikely. The business is growing steadily but within defined boundaries, not accelerating into a new phase.
metric_assessments.predictability: A 5/10 'Adequate' score reflects the reality that while franchise revenues are recurring and stable, consumer discretionary spending on quick-service restaurants varies with economic cycles—when job losses spike or confidence drops, traffic slows. Yum!'s mix of Taco Bell, KFC, and Pizza Hut franchises produces reasonably steady cash, but it lacks the recession-proof characteristics of essential services like utilities or staples like groceries. This volatility in demand is the core limitation preventing predictability from reaching 7+, even though the franchise model itself is structurally stable.
metric_assessments.valuation: An 'Adequate' 5/10 score indicates the stock is neither a bargain nor overpriced on a standalone basis—earnings multiples appear fair given the growth rate and profitability. What prevents a higher score is that valuation doesn't offer a compelling margin of safety; you're essentially paying a fair price for fair growth, leaving little cushion if execution stumbles or the market reassesses the consumer cyclical sector downward. For a beginner, this means no obvious bargain to jump on, but also not a warning to stay away.

quality_narrative.classification_reason: Yum! is rated Speculative because it combines solid profitability and cash generation with above-average leverage (4.39x debt-to-EBITDA) and exposure to consumer discretionary spending, which can weaken sharply in a recession. The business earns reliably and has iconic brands, but carries more debt than a defensive stock would, and growth is constrained—meaning the upside is limited while downside risk in a downturn is real. For beginners, this means Yum! is not a boring, stable core holding; it requires comfort with economic cycles and some balance-sheet risk.
quality_narrative.investment_narrative: Yum! Brands operates one of the world's most efficient franchise models, with a 31% return on invested capital and 20.5% profit margins that demonstrate how well the company extracts value from its iconic restaurant chains—KFC, Taco Bell, and Pizza Hut are global household names with loyal customers and franchisees who handle most day-to-day costs. The business generates double-digit revenue growth (15.2%) and earnings growth (11.4% projected over five years), backed by recurring franchise fees that are far more stable than typical restaurant stocks. However, the story has meaningful headwinds: the company carries moderate-to-high debt at 4.39x EBITDA, growth is bumping against saturation limits and won't accelerate further, and profitability is vulnerable to consumer spending cycles—when recessions hit, foot traffic declines. Valuation is neither cheap nor expensive, offering no margin of safety at current prices. For a beginner, Yum! is a quality franchisor with strong brands and cash flow, but it's best suited for investors comfortable with economic sensitivity and debt levels, and only as part of a diversified portfolio—not as a core, defensive holding.
quality_narrative.quality_conclusion: Yum! Brands, Inc. demonstrates mixed fundamental quality and shows particular strength in profitability, supported by a 30.92% ROIC.
quality_narrative.risk_profile: Key risks to watch: elevated leverage (Debt/EBITDA 4.4x); balance sheet constraints on growth.
quality_narrative.investment_summary: With 11.4% consensus EPS growth forecast, the growth outlook supports the investment case. The stock trades near estimated intrinsic value and is appropriate for investors with higher risk tolerance and conviction in the growth thesis.

_notes.returns_trajectory: Return on Equity (ROE) measures how much profit a company generates for every dollar of shareholder money invested. Return on Invested Capital (ROIC) is similar but also includes debt. Both are expressed as a percentage — higher is better. A company consistently earning above 15% ROIC is generally considered to have a genuine competitive advantage over its peers.
_notes.revenue_vs_ar: Accounts receivable is money the company is owed by customers who have not yet paid their bills. This chart compares revenue growth to accounts receivable growth each year. If accounts receivable is growing much faster than revenue, it may mean the company is struggling to collect payments — sometimes a sign of aggressive or fictitious revenue reporting. A healthy company should see both lines move at a similar pace.

_meta.type_rationale: FCF compounder — FCF margin 22.2%; DCF methods prioritised
_meta.method_explanation: Free cash flow compounders are businesses that consistently generate more cash than they need to run the business — think of companies like consumer goods brands or software firms with subscription revenue. Because their cash flows are reliable year after year, a Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model works very well. DCF asks: if I add up all the future cash flows this company will generate and adjust for the time value of money, what is it worth today? For these companies, that calculation gives a meaningful and trustworthy answer.

_meta.type_rationale: Cyclical business (basic materials) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
_meta.method_explanation: Cyclical companies — such as semiconductors, mining, or manufacturing — have profits that rise sharply in good times and fall sharply in downturns. This makes earnings-based methods like P/E unreliable, because the earnings figure at any single point in time can be unusually high or low. EV/EBITDA smooths out some of this distortion by looking at earnings before interest and tax, making it a fairer comparison across the economic cycle. Price-to-Sales is also useful as revenue tends to be more stable than profits for cyclical businesses.

company_profile.name: Yesway, Inc.
company_profile.type_rationale: Standard company — balanced weighting across all methods
company_profile.description: Yesway, Inc. owns and operates convenience stores in the United States. The company's store offers packaged bakery items, bottled water, salty snacks, bagged candy, jerky, chips, milk, bread, eggs, packaged beverages, meat snacks, chocolate, and nuts. It also offers food services, including deep-fried burritos. In addition, its store offers other ancillary services, including cash ATMs, crypto-currency ATMs, money orders, gift cards, propane tanks, in-store gaming, Amazon Lockers, lottery tickets, and other non-consumable products. It operates under two brands: Yesway and Allsup's. The company was founded in 2015 and is based in Fort Worth, Texas.
company_profile.description_summary:
Operates a chain of convenience stores across the United States under the Yesway and Allsup's brands, generating revenue primarily from selling packaged groceries, snacks, beverages, and prepared foods like deep-fried burritos. The stores also provide ancillary services including ATMs, money orders, lottery tickets, and Amazon Lockers.

What distinguishes Yesway is its diverse service offerings beyond typical convenience retail, notably cryptocurrency ATMs and in-store gaming, which generate additional revenue streams and enhance customer engagement.

final_score_pct = max(0, final_pct − penalty)

_notes.revenue_vs_net_income: This chart shows how much money the company brings in (revenue) versus how much it actually keeps as profit (net income) each year. A healthy company should show both lines growing over time. If revenue grows but net income shrinks, the company is spending more than it is earning — a warning sign worth investigating.
_notes.fcf_vs_debt: Free cash flow is the cash left over after the company has paid for everything it needs to run and grow the business. This chart compares that cash to the company's total debt. A company with rising free cash flow and falling debt is in a strong financial position. If debt is rising faster than cash, the company may be taking on more risk than it can comfortably manage.
_notes.shares_vs_buybacks: When a company buys back its own shares, it reduces the total number of shares in circulation — which means each remaining share represents a larger slice of the business. This chart shows how the share count has changed over time. Consistent buybacks are generally a sign that management believes the stock is undervalued and wants to return cash to shareholders.
_notes.returns_trajectory: Return on Equity (ROE) measures how much profit a company generates for every dollar of shareholder money invested. Return on Invested Capital (ROIC) is similar but also includes debt. Both are expressed as a percentage — higher is better. A company consistently earning above 15% ROIC is generally considered to have a genuine competitive advantage over its peers.
_notes.revenue_vs_ar: Accounts receivable is money the company is owed by customers who have not yet paid their bills. This chart compares revenue growth to accounts receivable growth each year. If accounts receivable is growing much faster than revenue, it may mean the company is struggling to collect payments — sometimes a sign of aggressive or fictitious revenue reporting. A healthy company should see both lines move at a similar pace.

company_profile.name: Yatra Online, Inc.
company_profile.type_rationale: Standard company — balanced weighting across all methods
company_profile.description: Yatra Online, Inc. operates as an online travel company in India and internationally. It operates through Air Ticketing; Hotels and Packages; and Other Services segments. The company offers travel-related services, including domestic and international air ticketing, hotel bookings, homestays, holiday packages, bus ticketing, rail ticketing, cab bookings, and ancillary services for leisure and business travelers. It also provides various services, such as exploring and searching comprises web and mobile platforms that enable customers to explore and search flights, hotels, holiday packages, buses, trains, and activities. In addition, the company offers tours, sightseeing, shows, and event services; rail and cab services; and other ancillary travel services, as well as sells travel vouchers and coupons. It serves its services through mobile applications comprising Yatra, a mobile interface; and Yatra Corporate, a self-booking application for business customers. Yatra Online, Inc. was incorporated in 2005 and is based in Gurugram, India.
company_profile.description_summary: This online travel platform operates across India and internationally, offering domestic and international air ticketing, hotel bookings, holiday packages, bus and rail ticketing, and cab services. Revenue derives primarily from air ticketing and hotel bookings, supplemented by ancillary travel services. The company serves leisure and business travelers through web and mobile platforms, including specialized corporate booking applications for business customers.
